title: The Macro Minute: How will the legislative process to extend tax cuts impact asset markets?

for Wednesday March 26 2025 as always
we'll start with the executive summary
from today's leadoff morning note if you
would like to review the analysis
supporting these conclusions and to see
if you need to make any changes in your
portfolio to remain on the right side of
Market risk need to be a client of 42
macro so today's ke macro question is
how will the legislative process to
extend and expand the Trump tax cuts
impact to asset markets our answers are
Republican leaders are nearing a deal to
extend and expand the 2017 tax cuts and
jobs act the signature legislative
Victory from president Trump's first
term and Hike the debt limit by $4
trillion per house Speaker Mike Johnson
who said the Senate is coming around to
the plan Senate Majority Leader John th
said there's consensus forming around
the $4 trillion do debt limit hike
despite opposition from Republican
senator Rand Paul Republican Senators
Josh Holley and Tommy tuberville opposed
the plan over potential Medicaid Cuts
with US Treasury expected to exhaust
cash by summer or early fall Republicans
aim toay pass tax cut legislation by
Memorial Day raising Against Time and
internal divisions th set a deadline of
the week of April 7th for the Senate to
pass a budget resolution needless to say
there remains a lot of execution risk
with respect to the legislative process
to extend and expand the Trump tax cuts
if the house gets its way there won't be
much money left over for the expand part
of the equation this means the positive
fisal impulse from the plan will be
limited in scope in our view this
scenario caps upside risk in Risk ass
assets associated with the passage of
the reconciliation Bill if however House
Republicans cave to the Senate
Republican strategy and adopt the
current policy Baseline to add trillions
more in deficit Finance tax cuts then we
could be on the brink of a l trust
moment in the treasury market the likely
dramatic widening of turn premum in the
bond market would initially be very
bearish for risk assets until it becomes
very bullish once the FED steps in and
provides the QE markets likely require
to avoid a global debt refinancing air
pocket this year
transitioning to our 42 micro dashboard
as always will wrap up with a question
from our community this one's regarding
um tax cuts says hi Dar the research is
quite extensive and impressive thank you
I was wondering have you looked at tax
cuts in the past from JFK Reagan and
George W bush to see tax receipts from
individuals and incomes and corporations
have increased or decreased three to
five years after the tax cuts so uh yes
and yes this is stuff I've studied
extensively throughout the course of my
career um so the key takeaway with
respect to tax cut
you know and and this is a very
political politicized sort of discussion
topic because on one side of the aisle
uh you're blowing holes in the budget
deficit on the other side of the a
you're spurring growth uh and and and
raising uh tax revenues and and like
everything with respect to politics the
answerers usually uh somewhere in the
middle so let me draw a diagram to kind
of illustrate uh what I mean by this so
uh and so I see the key takeaway with
respect to tax cuts is you know know the
economy grows nominally you know we have
population growth we have productivity
growth uh you know those factors of
production generally cause the economy
to expand credit is generally expanding
obviously if you have a budget deficit
that's growing over time that's going to
be contributing to economic activity uh
as well so uh you generally have to
expect that we're not going to see tax
receipts from income or from individuals
and corporations ever really decline
unless you're in a recession you need to
see like a significant draw down in
nominal GDP growth to see tax tax
receipts uh decline on on let's say a
year-over-year basis so in terms of how
tax receipts actually impact the budget
and then ultimately impact the economy
uh let's use this um diagram here on the
chart on the right ignore the actual um
ignore the actual uh uh sort of Legend
on the chart or the the T the the um the
headline on the chart I'm just going to
use the lines on the chart to illustrate
what I'm trying to show here so imagine
so what a tax cut is is the negative
deviation from the Baseline of projected
tax receipt growth uh and that negative
deviation which would be the area under
the curve between the red line and the
orange line is what causes economic
growth is is what is what is
contributory to economic growth to the
extent that the the the the the saved
the the the the savings ultimately get
filtered uh into and throughout the
economy and so let's imagine hey we're
on this path here the red line for tax
currently based on the extent the the
the deadline of the uh uh tax cust and
jobs acts expiring on December 31st
let's say this is where how tax receipts
would have grown in that scenario the
Orange Line scenario was effectively how
tax receipts will grow if they expand
the Trump tax cuts uh Beyond December
31st of this year and then the Blue Line
in this chart uh sorry my pin the Blue
Line in this chart would be what would
happen if they expanded uh the Trump tax
cuts notice how the the slope of each of
these lines is positively sloped so tax
receipts will go up and what they'll
tell you on Fox news or Fox Business is
that you know hey see we cut taxes and
tax receipts go go went up and of course
that will be true but the reality is is
they will go up less fast than they were
initially supposed to relative to
current law which will ultimately cause
the budget deficit to be larger in the
future than it is currently projected to
be by uh most um governmental and
non-governmental agencies particularly
the bipartisan agencies like the CBO the
the committee for responsible federal
budget the Yale budget lab the pin W
model all these uh bipartisan think
tanks believe that uh extending the
Trump tax cuts will knock us to a lower
slope with lower positive slope with
respect to Growing tax receipts and then
they obviously also think that expanding
the Trump tax cuts will loock us to an
even no more even less positive slope
and so that's kind of how the tax uh cut
policy works and so going back to the
question you know when you go back and
you study tax cuts in history it's easy
to get caught up in the fact that
receipts continue to rise in the future
and what's missing from that kind of
analysis I would considered to be
politically lazy analysis is where would
tax receipts have been had you not
implemented these tax cuts but also on
the other side of that where would
economic growth have been had we not
implemented these tax cuts you know so
there is a trade-off there is a balance
and generally speaking when you go
forward and backwards in time we don't
have the the fiscal concerns and the and
the constraints that we currently have
today you know I would argue that you
know tax cuts from JFK and Ronald Reagan
were fantastic ideas given the low level
of deficits and debt that we had in the
economy much less so with respect to
George Bush in terms of the level of
debt but we did start from a budget
surplus at that time so you can make the
case that it was a great idea now we are
starting with a deficit to GDP of 7% of
GDP which is a record non-w non-
recession budget deficit and we're
starting with a Deb to GDP ratio that's
over 100 and a debt to liquidity ratio
that's already over one which is well
north of where peer economies are so
these are issues in these deficit issues
are essentially much bigger now that the
impact of growing the budget deficit and
growing debt there's a much greater risk
as it relates to the treasury crowding
out financial markets and crowding out
economic activity from growing the
budget Deb from debt Finance tax cuts
and that's the issue you can Finance tax
cuts you know with economic growth and
that's essentially what they're going to
argue that they're doing but the reality
is is what we learned from the previous
uh uh uh ta Trump tax cuts and jobs Act
is that yes it was positive for economic
growth but it wasn't positive enough for
economic growth to prevent the deficit
from widening and and in our opinion uh
the same Dynamic is likely to occur here
just given the um the starting point so
